one_line_takeaway: A rigorous survey that gives AI-safety evaluation a shared vocabulary and a three-axis taxonomy (capabilities x propensities x control; behavioral x internal techniques; technical x governance frameworks), arguing that saturating benchmarks must give way to threat-model-driven evaluations that can never prove capability absence.
key_finding: The central synthesized thesis is that no single benchmark or behavioral test can establish safety, because (i) benchmarks measure performance not capability and saturate rapidly (illustrated by o3 jumping from <2% to 25.2% on FrontierMath), (ii) risks emerge combinatorially from capability interactions (e.g. situational awareness + deception enabling different behavior under evaluation vs deployment), and (iii) there is a fundamental asymmetry -- evaluations can confirm the presence of a capability but can never prove its absence. The paper argues safety therefore requires layering the three property classes, complementing behavioral evidence with internal/interpretability methods, and gating scaling decisions on evaluation results within governance frameworks, while remaining alert to sandbagging and safetywashing.
core_contribution: This is a systematic literature review (Chapter 5 of the 'AI Safety Atlas' textbook by Grey & Segerie, CeSIA), not an empirical study. Its own contribution is a synthesizing taxonomy that organizes the scattered field of AI safety evaluation along three axes: (1) WHAT properties to measure -- capabilities (upper bounds on what a model can do when maximally elicited), propensities (what it tends to do by default), and control (whether safety measures hold when the model actively subverts them); (2) HOW to measure -- behavioral/black-box techniques (standard prompting, red teaming, best-of-N sampling, supervised fine-tuning, scaffolding/elicitation) versus internal/gray-box techniques (linear probes, sparse autoencoders, mechanistic interpretability, representation engineering); and (3) HOW measurements integrate into frameworks -- technical frameworks (the Model Organisms approach, e.g. Anthropic's sleeper agents) and governance frameworks (Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policies/ASL levels, OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework). The paper argues that benchmarks alone are insufficient and must be superseded by evaluations that work backward from concrete threat models, and it consolidates definitions, examples, and open challenges into a single reference.
threat_model: The paper is explicitly organized around advanced/frontier AI risk (this is a genuine security framing supplied by the paper, not imposed). Concretely: (1) dangerous CAPABILITIES -- cyber-offense/vulnerability discovery and exploitation, deception, persuasion/manipulation, weapons (CBRN) acquisition, autonomous replication/self-proliferation, long-horizon planning, situational awareness, and AI-development capability (per Shevlane et al. 2023); (2) concerning PROPENSITIES -- power-seeking, scheming, sycophancy, deception; and (3) an adversarial/scheming AI in the CONTROL agenda that deliberately tries to subvert safety measures (distinct from human-driven misuse like prompt injection or data poisoning -- the paper stresses the AI itself is the adversary). Additional adversarial/systemic threats to the evaluation process itself: model 'sandbagging' (strategic underperformance to appear below regulatory thresholds) and organizational 'safetywashing' (rebranding capability gains as safety progress).
limitations: As a review it inherits and foregrounds the field's limitations rather than solving them: (1) proving ABSENCE of capabilities is impossible; (2) measurement sensitivity -- performance can swing up to 76% from few-shot format changes and ~5 percentage points from trivial option-label changes, undermining reproducibility; (3) curse of dimensionality -- combinatorial explosion of scenarios makes comprehensive coverage intractable; (4) resource cost -- e.g. >100,000 API calls to assess one benchmark, and human control groups needing 568 hours for just 7 R&D tasks (Wijk et al. 2024); (5) behavioral testing's blind spots pending interpretability progress; (6) emergent/unanticipated capabilities (Voyager/Minecraft; Gemini zero-shot Kalamang translation from an in-context grammar book); (7) systemic incentive failures -- sandbagging (demonstrated for Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, o1) and safetywashing. As a document it is a curated secondary source (a textbook chapter, ~mid-2025 snapshot), so it offers no new empirical validation and its currency will decay as the field moves.

datasets_used: No datasets used by the authors (survey). Benchmarks/datasets CATALOGUED and discussed include: MMLU (57 subjects), GPQA, BigBench, GLUE/SuperGLUE, SWAG/HellaSwag, GSM8K, MATH, MGSM, FrontierMath, Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), APPS, HumanEval/HumanEval-XL/HumanEval-V, BigCode, ETHICS, TruthfulQA, AgentHarm, MACHIAVELLI (>500,000 scenarios), WMDP (3,668 MCQs across bio/cyber/chemical), MNIST (70,000 digits), CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet (1.2M images, 1,000 categories), ARC/ConceptARC, RE-Bench (METR)
quantitative_results: FrontierMath: SOTA models solved <2% (Nov 2024), OpenAI o3 later reached 25.2% (cited, illustrating benchmark saturation)
Reversal Curse (Berglund et al. 2024): GPT-4 ~79% accuracy on forward relations vs ~33% on logically-equivalent reverses

Capabilities-correlation thresholds (Ren et al. 2024): >70% correlation with general capability = likely not measuring safety distinctly; <40% = distinct; TruthfulQA correlates >80% with general capabilities
Prompt-format sensitivity: up to 76% performance variation from few-shot formatting; ~5 percentage-point swings from option-label/bracket/whitespace changes
Governance scaling buffers: DeepMind FSF re-evaluates every 6x effective compute and every 3 months of fine-tuning; Anthropic RSP every 4x effective compute
ARC Prize 2024: compute-restricted ($10) and unrestricted ($10,000) tracks both reached ~55% accuracy
Resource cost: >100,000 API calls to assess a single benchmark; human control group spent 568 hours on 7 R&D tasks (Wijk et al. 2024)
OpenAI Preparedness thresholds: only post-mitigation 'medium' or below may be deployed; 'high' or below may be developed further
baselines_compared: Not an experimental paper -- no model baselines run by the authors. The paper's own comparative analysis contrasts governance frameworks: Anthropic RSP/ASL levels (ASL-1 through ASL-3+, biosafety-inspired, overall model tiers) vs OpenAI Preparedness Framework (per-risk-category low-to-critical spectrum, pre- vs post-mitigation, 'unknown unknowns' focus) vs DeepMind Frontier Safety Framework (domain-specific Critical Capability Levels). It also contrasts benchmarks vs evaluations, and behavioral vs internal evaluation techniques.
